games.explanation: Glasnow strikes out the world but he also walks the world, and a price implying Houston gets blown out ignores that he hands out free passes at a clip that keeps every game close. The Astros draw enough walks and put the ball in play often enough to turn his command wobbles into actual innings, and McCullers, ugly peripherals and all, only needs to keep pace under a closed roof. Glasnow is issuing free passes to nearly 13% of hitters he faces, which is the kind of number that turns favorites into coin flips no matter how the strikeouts look on the back of the card. Getting plus money on a game our numbers see as a near tossup is the entire angle, and +184 prices Houston like they don't belong on the field.

Seattle being favored here is a habit more than an analysis, because Bryan Woo is very good and Atlanta lost the plot months ago, but the price has drifted past where the actual matchup lives. Woo runs into trouble against right-handed bats specifically, and Olson and Baldwin are exactly the loud-contact profiles that turn his fly-ball tendencies into damage. Perez is the obvious wart, except he only needs to keep pace in a park that suppresses runs by roughly 17 percent with the roof shut, and the projected score is essentially a coin flip. Getting plus money on the better roster in a low-total environment is the entire angle, and +120 is generous for a game the math sees closer to 58-42.

Bailey Ober's reputation runs ahead of his actual results against right-handed bats, and Washington happens to roll out a lineup full of them. Wood is a nightmare matchup for this exact arm profile, with a barrel rate north of 17% against righties, and Abrams and García both do their damage from the same side of the box. Mikolas is no prize, but the gap between these two starters is narrower than the price suggests, and the Twins lineup outside of Buxton is more name than production. Getting +116 on the team with the better pitching matchup top-to-bottom is the kind of math the market keeps fumbling.

Skenes is the best pitcher on the planet right now and the price still has to acknowledge that the team behind him is, in fact, the Pirates. Arizona is sending a left-handed-heavy order at him, and Skenes has been meaningfully more human against lefties than righties this year, with Marte and Carroll the exact barrel-rate profiles that punish the rare mistake. Pittsburgh's offense, meanwhile, is a strikeout machine that has to manufacture every run it scores, and a closed roof in the desert does nothing to change that. The sim sees a 4-4 game where the dog wins 54% of the time, and the board is paying you plus money to take it.
